summary: Joe Reis argues that data professionals who dismiss AI are like Flat Earthers, clinging to failing approaches despite a tool that could dramatically improve the industry's dismal 20% project success rate. He contends that AI can compress weeks of work into hours for tasks like data modeling, code migrations, and stakeholder analysis, and that resistance often stems from protecting budgets and kingdoms rather than legitimate technical concerns.
key_points: Data industry project success rates have hovered around 20-30% for decades
AI critics often haven't tried newer models or techniques like RAG
AI can dramatically accelerate data modeling by parsing stakeholder interviews
Some resistance to AI is about protecting budgets and job security, not technical merit
The expectation is shifting from slides to working MVPs delivered in hours
money_quotes: AI denialists are the data industry's Flat Earthers.
If your child continuously had failing grades with an average of a 20% pass rate from elementary school through high school, you'd question many things - you, your kid, the school system, etc.
Nobody can wait several months to add a column to a database. Those days are over.
I read between the lines and realized this was more about keeping his job and growing his kingdom's budget. So it goes.
The other thing AI allows is 'show, don't tell.' It's not enough to show slides and diagrams.
key_insight: The data industry's chronic underperformance makes AI adoption not just beneficial but necessary, and those resisting it are often motivated by self-interest rather than sound technical judgment.
